problem_summary: Flashlight produces no light. Most likely cause is dead batteries or corroded contacts, both of which are field-repairable without tools.
visible_observations: No light output on any brightness setting
Unit feels lighter than expected — possible dead or missing batteries
Possible white or green residue visible inside battery compartment

likely_causes.evidence: No light on any setting; unit has not been tested recently or was left on accidentally

likely_causes.description: Corroded battery contacts blocking electrical connection
likely_causes.evidence: White or green powder visible on battery terminals or inside compartment

likely_causes.description: Faulty or stuck switch not completing the circuit
likely_causes.evidence: Switch feels mushy, does not click, or feels mechanically stuck

likely_causes.description: Blown LED driver or failed LED module
likely_causes.evidence: Fresh batteries installed, contacts clean, switch clicks firmly — still no light

clarifying_questions: Have you tried fresh batteries from a sealed, unused pack?
Is there any visible white powder or green residue inside the battery compartment?
Does the power switch click with a firm tactile feel, or does it feel loose or stuck?
tools_needed: Fresh replacement batteries (correct size for flashlight model)
White vinegar and cotton swabs (corrosion cleaning)
next_best_test: Open the battery compartment and inspect contacts for corrosion. Install fresh batteries from a sealed pack and test immediately.

step_by_step_repair.instruction: Open the battery compartment. Inspect the metal contacts for white or green corrosion deposits.
step_by_step_repair.warning: Do not touch battery acid or heavy corrosion with bare skin — use gloves or a cloth.
step_by_step_repair.expected_outcome: Clean contacts mean batteries are the issue. Corrosion present means contacts need cleaning before new batteries will work.

step_by_step_repair.instruction: If corrosion is present, dampen a cotton swab with white vinegar. Scrub contacts until residue is removed. Let dry fully before installing batteries.
step_by_step_repair.expected_outcome: Contacts turn shiny silver — corrosion neutralized.

step_by_step_repair.instruction: Install fresh batteries with correct polarity (match + and - markings inside compartment).
step_by_step_repair.expected_outcome: Flashlight turns on immediately. If not, proceed to next step.

step_by_step_repair.instruction: Press and hold the switch firmly for 3 seconds while testing. Check if the switch feels sticky or fails to click.
step_by_step_repair.expected_outcome: Light activates if switch was sticking. Continued failure points to LED driver fault.

stop_conditions: Stop if batteries show swelling, leaking liquid, or unusual heat — dispose safely per local regulations, do not force into compartment.
Stop if you see burn marks or melted plastic inside the battery compartment — unit is damaged and should be replaced.
prevention: Remove batteries from emergency flashlights stored for more than 3 months to prevent corrosion damage.
Test all emergency equipment monthly and log the test date on a label attached to the unit.
Store a sealed spare battery pack with each critical emergency flashlight.
